title: Single 20-somethings using AI to make first move on dating apps, says Hinge boss
summary:
Single 20-somethings using AI to start conversations on dating apps, as Hinge boss, Jackie Jantos, revealed in a BBC interview. Despite the lack of confidence among older generations, they are seeking "love" and are struggling to find the confidence to engage in long-term relationships. They argue that AI tools like Hinge's prompts help them express themselves more authentically.

Jantos, speaking from experience, believes Hinge's AI is more about helping users express themselves than about writing words for others. She warns that this feature could lead to "dating app burnout" and a return to more organic, in-person interactions.

Hinge, founded in 2012 and owned by Match Group, has grown its user base by targeting younger demographics. It has become the most-used dating app in the UK, with Tinder and Grindr leading the charge. However, usage has declined, dropping from 1.5 million in May 2025 to 1.4 million in the same period.

The boss of Hinge emphasized the importance of staying true to the platform's mission of "deleting" users, highlighting its commitment to authenticity and long-term relationships.

Summary: 
Hinge, a dating app, uses AI to create first-move conversations on its platform. The AI features are designed to help users express interest in a match, offering a "helping" experience rather than a "writing" message. Hinge has grown significantly since its inception, with a focus on user engagement and a commitment to long-term relationships. The app has expanded its user base, with over 1.5 million active users in the year following May 2025. Hinge has also introduced a new dating service, Tinder, which has seen a decline in user count over the past three years.

attempts.summary:
Single 20-somethings using AI to make first move on dating apps, says Hinge boss
Health
Video
Single 20-somethings are using AI to start conversations on dating apps because they lack the confidence of older generations, says the boss of Hinge.
Jackie Jantos told the BBC Gen Z daters "absolutely want love" but were "struggling to have the confidence to put themselves out there" as they socialise less in person.
She defended Hinge's AI feature which creates prompts to start chatting with a match as "not about writing words for you" but "helping you express who you are".
Hinge has continued to grow its UK users despite some relationship experts warning of "dating app burnout" and a return to more organic in person meetings.
Founded in 2012 and owned by Match Group, which also owns Tinder and Match.com, Hinge has built its brand around the slogan "designed to be deleted".
Jantos dismisses accusations that this is "just a marketing line", saying it wants to help users find long-term relationships rather stay on the platform indefinitely.
Tinder is the most visited dating app, but over the past three years usage has been dropping and it's now only marginally ahead of nearest competitor Hinge. Bumble and Grindr follow Hinge in the most used dating services.
Some 1.

Single 20-somethings using AI to make first move on dating apps, says Hinge boss
Health
Video
Single 20-somethings using AI to make first move on dating apps - Hinge boss
6 days ago
Yasmin Rufo
Sean Farrington
presenter, Big Boss Interview podcast
Emer Moreau
Single 20-somethings are using AI to start conversations on dating apps because they lack the confidence of older generations, says the boss of Hinge.
Jackie Jantos told the BBC Gen Z daters "absolutely want love" but were "struggling to have the confidence to put themselves out there" as they socialise less in person.
She defended Hinge's AI feature which creates prompts to start chatting with a match as "not about writing words for you" but "helping you express who you are".
Hinge has continued to grow its UK users despite some relationship experts warning of "dating app burnout" and a return to more organic in person meetings.
Founded in 2012 and owned by Match Group, which also owns Tinder and Match.com, Hinge has built its brand around the slogan "designed to be deleted".
Jantos dismisses accusations that this is "just a marketing line", saying it wants to help users find long-term relationships rather stay on the platform indefinitely.
Tinder is the most visited dating app, but over the past three years usage has been dropping and it's now only marginally ahead of nearest competitor Hinge. Bumble and Grindr follow Hinge in the most used dating services.
Some 1.5 million adults used Hinge in the year up to May 2025, up from 1.4 million a year earlier.
Over the same period, Tinder's audience fell from 1.9 million to 1.5 million, according to Ipsos iris data.
